subject: mathematics

sections.title: Patterns, Properties, and a Pretty Procedure!

prose_summary:
Section 11.1 recalls factors and primes and applies prime factorisation to compute the Highest Common Factor. It opens with Sameeksha tiling a 12 ft × 16 ft room with the largest possible whole-number square tile: the side must be a common factor of 12 and 16, whose common factors are 1, 2, 4, so the largest tile is 4 ft. This motivates the definition of the Highest Common Factor (HCF), also called the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD), as the greatest of the common factors, reinforced by Lekhana packing 84 kg and 108 kg of rice into equal whole-number bags and by the Grade 6 'Jump Jackpot' game, where the longest jump landing on both treasure numbers equals their HCF. Because listing all factors is cumbersome and error-prone for numbers with many factors, the section revisits primes — a number greater than 1 whose only factors are 1 and itself — and prime factorisation, establishing through examples such as 90 = 3×3×2×5 that the resulting prime factors are always the same regardless of the order in which the factorising is done, and introduces a compact division method for finding a prime factorisation (for example 105 = 3×5×7 and 30 = 2×3×5). It then shows how every factor of a number is a 'subpart' of its prime factorisation — reordering 840 = 2×2×2×3×5×7 shows 28 is a factor with co-factor 30 — and systematically lists all factors of 225 from its primes. Anshu's claim that larger numbers have longer prime factorisations is introduced as a conjecture and disproved by a counterexample (121 = 11×11). Worked Examples 1–5 find common factors and the HCF of 45 and 75, 112 and 84, 96 and 275, 30 and 72, and 225 and 750, leading to the rule: take each common prime the minimum number of times it occurs across the numbers.

Section 11.2 recalls multiples and applies prime factorisation to compute the Lowest Common Multiple. It opens with Anshu and Guna making torans from 6 cm and 8 cm cloth strips: the shortest common length is a common multiple of 6 and 8, and the smallest, 24 cm, is their Lowest Common Multiple (LCM), the least of the common multiples. A sweet-shop gajak problem (free every 7 days for the shop's Monday cycle, visited every 10 days) gives the LCM of 7 and 10 as 70 days, and the Grade 6 'Idli-Vada' game reinforces that the first common call-out is the LCM. Since listing multiples is tedious for larger numbers, the section develops the prime-factorisation method: comparing a number with its multiple shows the multiple's factorisation contains the number's, so a common multiple must contain each number's prime subpart. Worked Example 6 (14 and 35 → 70) and Example 7 (96 and 360 → 1440) build the LCM by taking each prime the maximum number of times it occurs in either factorisation, and the method extends to more than two numbers.

Section 11.3 reasons about patterns and properties relating HCF and LCM and applies an efficient combined procedure. It observes that when one number is a factor of the other, the HCF is the smaller number, and formalises this as a general statement, introducing the terms 'general statement' and 'generalisation' and describing such pairs with algebra (n and 5n, with HCF = n). Figure it Out sets ask students to make and explain general statements about the HCF and LCM of consecutive even, consecutive odd, even, consecutive, co-prime numbers, and multiples of 3. The section explores what happens to the HCF when both numbers are doubled (it doubles, since each gains a factor of 2), and when both numbers are multiples of a common number. It also examines pairs where both numbers are multiples of the same number (such as 14×6 and 14×9, whose HCF is 42) and asks when the HCF equals the common multiplier. It presents an efficient division procedure that divides both numbers by common primes simultaneously until the quotients share no common factor, reads off the HCF from the column of divisors and the LCM from the divisors together with the final quotients, and shows Guna's and Anshu's shortcuts of dividing by larger common factors (such as 50, or 10 then 7) all at once, noting one need not divide by a single prime at a time. It then establishes the key property HCF × LCM = product of the two numbers by examining how common and non-common prime factors distribute between the HCF and the LCM, and asks whether it extends to three numbers. Its extensive Figure it Out set applies all of this to repeating colour patterns, factor-and-multiple checks, HCF/LCM stated as prime factorisations, a cowherd folklore problem from Karnataka, cube-packing, divisibility puzzles including 'Fire in the Mountain' and a dog-and-rabbit chase, and a fraction-sum problem posed by the Indian mathematician Mahaviracharya (850 CE).

enumerated_worked_examples.description: Find the common factors and HCF of 45 and 75. Using 45 = 3×3×5 and 75 = 3×5×5, the common subparts are 3, 5, 15, so common factors are 1, 3, 5, 15 and HCF = 15.
Find the common factors and HCF of 112 and 84. Using 112 = 2×2×2×2×7 and 84 = 2×2×3×7, the largest common subpart is 2×2×7 = 28, so HCF = 28.
Find the common factors and HCF of 96 and 275. Since 96 = 2×2×2×2×2×3 and 275 = 5×5×11 share no prime subpart, the only common factor is 1, which is also the HCF.
Find the HCF of 30 and 72 directly. From 30 = 2×3×5 and 72 = 2×2×2×3×3, take each common prime the minimum number of times (one 2, one 3): HCF = 2×3 = 6.
Find the HCF of 225 and 750. From 225 = 3×3×5×5 and 750 = 2×3×5×5×5, take one 3 and two 5s: HCF = 3×5×5 = 75.
Find the LCM of 14 and 35. From 14 = 2×7 and 35 = 5×7, the LCM must contain 2×7 and 5×7 as subparts: the lowest is 2×5×7 = 70.
Find the LCM of 96 and 360. From 96 = 2×2×2×2×2×3 and 360 = 2×2×2×3×3×5, take the maximum occurrences of each prime (five 2s, two 3s, one 5): LCM = 1440.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: section 11.1 p.48
enumerated_exercises.description: 'Jump Jackpot' game: for treasures on (a) 14 and 30; (b) 7 and 11; (c) 30 and 50; (d) 28 and 42, find the longest jump size from 0 that lands on both numbers, and explain why it equals their HCF.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 11.1 p.51
enumerated_exercises.description: List all the factors of (a) 90; (b) 105; (c) 132; (d) 360 (24 factors); (e) 840 (32 factors), using prime factorisation.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 11.1 p.53
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the common factors and HCF of (a) 50, 60; (b) 140, 275; (c) 77, 725; (d) 370, 592; (e) 81, 243.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 11.1 p.54
Figure it Out Q2, section 11.1 p.54
enumerated_exercises.description: Given 72 = 6×12 and 144 = 8×18, decide whether one can conclude the two numbers have no common factor other than 1, and explain why not.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: section 11.2 p.56
enumerated_exercises.description: 'Idli-Vada' game: for the number pairs (a) 4 and 6; (b) 7 and 11; (c) 14 and 30; (d) 15 and 55, find the first number for which 'idli-vada' is called out, and decide whether the answer is always the LCM.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 11.2 p.58
Figure it Out Q1, section 11.3 p.59
enumerated_exercises.description: Make and explain a general statement about the HCF of (a) two consecutive even numbers; (b) two consecutive odd numbers; (c) two even numbers; (d) two consecutive numbers; (e) two co-prime numbers.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 11.3 p.59
enumerated_exercises.description: Given that the LCM of 3 and 24 is 24: (a) find more pairs where the LCM is one of the two numbers; (b) make a general statement about such numbers and describe them using algebra.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 11.3 p.59
enumerated_exercises.description: Make and explain a general statement about the LCM of (a) two multiples of 3; (b) two consecutive even numbers; (c) two consecutive numbers; (d) two co-prime numbers.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: section 11.3 p.62
enumerated_exercises.description: For (a) 45, 105; (b) 275, 352; (c) 222, 370, explore whether the LCM is a factor of the product, find the multiplier that gives the product, and observe that it is the HCF (suggesting HCF × LCM = product).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 11.3 p.63
enumerated_exercises.description: In two rows of repeating coloured stars, determine when the blue stars will next meet.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 11.3 p.63
Figure it Out Q3, section 11.3 p.63
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the HCF and LCM (stating answers as prime factorisations) of (a) 3×3×5×7×7 and 12×7×11; (b) 45 and 36.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 11.3 p.63
enumerated_exercises.description: Find two numbers whose HCF is 1 and LCM is 66.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 11.3 p.63
enumerated_exercises.description: Karnataka folklore problem: a cowherd's cows pass in equal groups through crossings of 3, 5, and 7 gates; with fewer than 200 cows, find how many cows he had.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 11.3 p.64
enumerated_exercises.description: A box is 12 cm × 18 cm × 36 cm; decide which cubes of side (a) 9 cm; (b) 6 cm; (c) 4 cm; (d) 3 cm; (e) 2 cm can be packed without gaps.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q7, section 11.3 p.64
enumerated_exercises.description: Choose the largest number that perfectly divides both 306 and 36 from (a) 36; (b) 612; (c) 18; (d) 3; (e) 2; (f) 360.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q8, section 11.3 p.64
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the smallest number divisible by 3, 4, 5, and 7 that leaves a remainder of 10 when divided by 11.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q9, section 11.3 p.64
enumerated_exercises.description: In 'Fire in the Mountain', no one is out for 6 or 9 but some are out for 10; choose how many children could have been playing initially from (a) 72; (b) 90; (c) 45; (d) 3; (e) 36; (f) None of these.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q10, section 11.3 p.64
enumerated_exercises.description: Tick the correct statement(s) about the LCM of two different primes (m, n): (a) less than both; (b) between the two; (c) greater than both; (d) less than m×n; (e) greater than m×n.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q11, section 11.3 p.64
enumerated_exercises.description: A dog chasing a rabbit with a 150 ft head start jumps 9 ft each time the rabbit jumps 7 ft; find in how many leaps the dog catches up.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q12, section 11.3 p.64
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the smallest number that is a multiple of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q13, section 11.3 p.64
enumerated_exercises.description: Mahaviracharya's problem (850 CE): add 8/15, 1/20, 7/36, 11/63, and 1/21, and find an efficient way to compute the sum (using the LCM of the denominators).
